title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Philadelphia’s Indego bike share system faces the same operational challenge as every bike share system: rebalancing bikes to meet anticipated demand.
Imagine you’re an Indego operations manager at 6:00 AM on a Monday morning. You have: - 200 stations across Philadelphia - Limited trucks and staff for moving bikes - 2-3 hours before morning rush hour demand peaks - The question: Which stations will run out of bikes by 8:30 AM?
This lab will teach you to build predictive models that forecast bike share demand across space (different stations) and time (different hours) to help solve this operational problem.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(sf)
library(tigris)
library(tidycensus)

library(knitr)
library(patchwork)
library(kableExtra)
library(here)

options(scipen = 999) # Get rid of scientific notation to make it look tidier

  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.x   = element_text(size = 10, angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y   = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title    = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "#D0D0D0", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks    = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

mapTheme <- theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8),

Pause here Look at the data. What do you suspect the coordinate system is? What do you notice about the date and time structure?

The coordinate fields suggest that the data uses geographic coordinates in latitude and longitude, likely in decimal degrees (such as WGS84). The time variables are stored as full timestamps with both date and time, rather than date alone. Because each trip has a start and end timestamp, the data will likely need to be aggregated to hourly intervals for station-hour analysis.

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Create Time Bins
We need to aggregate trips into hourly intervals for our panel data structure. Read up on the lubridate library for more information on the date/time data extraction.


Code
indego <- indego %>%
  mutate(
    # Parse datetime
    start_datetime = mdy_hm(start_time),
    end_datetime = mdy_hm(end_time),
    
    # Create hourly bins
    interval60 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"),
    
    # Extract time features
    week = week(interval60),
    month = month(interval60, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval60),
    date = as.Date(interval60),
    
    # Create useful indicators
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )

# Look at temporal features
head(indego %>% select(start_datetime, interval60, week, dotw, hour, weekend))

Question Explain the interval60 feature to someone else at your table.

interval60 is an hourly time bin created from the trip start time. It rounds each trip down to the start of the hour, so trips that begin within the same hour are grouped together. This is useful because the analysis predicts bike demand at the station-hour level rather than for individual trips.

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Trips Over Time


Code
# Daily trip counts
daily_trips <- indego %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarize(trips = n())

ggplot(daily_trips, aes(x = date, y = trips)) +
  geom_line(color = "#3182bd", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Indego Daily Ridership - Q2 2025",
    subtitle = "Spring to early summer demand patterns in Philadelphia",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Daily Trips",
    caption = "Source: Indego bike share"
  ) +
  plotTheme









Question: What patterns do you see? How does ridership change over time?
The plot suggests that Indego ridership rises overall during Q2 2025. The red smoothed line increases from April through June, indicating stronger demand as the quarter progresses. In addition, the blue daily series shows regular up-and-down variation, which suggests a repeating weekly cycle in bike use. There are also several unusually low-ridership days, which may reflect weekends, weather conditions, or other one-off disruptions.

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Hourly Patterns


Code
# Average trips by hour and day type
hourly_patterns <- indego %>%
  group_by(hour, weekend) %>%
  summarize(avg_trips = n() / n_distinct(date)) %>%
  mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday"))

ggplot(hourly_patterns, aes(x = hour, y = avg_trips, color = day_type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Hourly Ridership Patterns",
    subtitle = "Clear commute patterns on weekdays",
    x = "Hour of Day",
    y = "Average Trips per Hour",
    color = "Day Type"
  ) +
  plotTheme









Question: When are the peak hours? How do weekends differ from weekdays?
The busiest hours on weekdays are around 8 AM and especially 4–6 PM, with the strongest peak at 5 PM. In contrast, weekends do not show a sharp morning peak. Instead, weekend ridership builds gradually and remains high from around noon through the late afternoon. This suggests a commuter pattern on weekdays and a more leisure-oriented pattern on weekends.

Question These data are in WGS84. If you wanted to calculate a spatial distance-based variable, like a buffer, should you use the dataset as it?
No. WGS84 stores coordinates in latitude and longitude (degrees), so it is not ideal for calculating distance-based measures such as buffers. For spatial distance calculations, the data should first be projected into a coordinate system with linear units such as meters or feet.

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Join Census Data to Stations
We’ll spatially join census characteristics to each bike station.


Code
# Create sf object for stations
stations_sf <- indego %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatial join to get census tract for each station
stations_census <- st_join(stations_sf, philly_census, left = TRUE) %>%
  st_drop_geometry()

# Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts.

stations_for_map <- indego %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc))

# Add back to trip data
indego_census <- indego %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census %>% 
      select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, 
             Percent_White, Total_Pop),
    by = "start_station"
  )


# Prepare data for visualization
stations_for_map <- indego %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc))

# Create the map showing problem stations
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "lightyellow",
    high = "darkgreen",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar,
    na.value = "grey90"
  ) +
  # Stations with census data (small grey dots)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map %>% filter(has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map %>% filter(!has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)",
    caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts"
  ) +
  mapTheme

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Dealing with missing data
We need to decide what to do with the non-residential bike share stations. For this example, we are going to remove them – this is not necessarily the right way to do things always, but for the sake of simplicity, we are narrowing our scope to only stations in residential neighborhoods. We might opt to create a separate model for non-residential stations..


Code
# Identify which stations to keep
valid_stations <- stations_census %>%
  filter(!is.na(Med_Inc)) %>%
  pull(start_station)

# Filter trip data to valid stations only
indego_census <- indego %>%
  filter(start_station %in% valid_stations) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census %>% 
      select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, 
             Percent_White, Total_Pop),
    by = "start_station"
  )

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Get Weather Data
Weather significantly affects bike share demand! Let’s get hourly weather for Philadelphia.


Code
# Get weather from Philadelphia International Airport (KPHL)
# This covers Q1 2025: January 1 - March 31
library(riem)
weather_data <- riem_measures(
  station = "PHL",  # Philadelphia International Airport
  date_start = "2025-04-01",
  date_end = "2025-06-30"
)

# Process weather data
weather_processed <- weather_data %>%
  mutate(
    interval60 = floor_date(valid, unit = "hour"),
    Temperature = tmpf,  # Temperature in Fahrenheit
    Precipitation = ifelse(is.na(p01i), 0, p01i),  # Hourly precip in inches
    Wind_Speed = sknt  # Wind speed in knots
  ) %>%
  select(interval60, Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed) %>%
  distinct(interval60, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Check for missing hours and interpolate if needed
weather_complete <- weather_processed %>%
  complete(interval60 = seq(min(interval60), max(interval60), by = "hour")) %>%
  fill(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed, .direction = "down")

# Look at the weather
summary(weather_complete %>% select(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed))

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Create Complete Panel Structure
Not every station has trips every hour. We need a complete panel where every station-hour combination exists (even if Trip_Count = 0).


Code
# Calculate expected panel size
n_stations <- length(unique(trips_panel$start_station))
n_hours <- length(unique(trips_panel$interval60))
expected_rows <- n_stations * n_hours

cat("Expected panel rows:", format(expected_rows, big.mark = ","), "\n")


Expected panel rows: 548,604 


Code
cat("Current rows:", format(nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n")


Current rows: 179,107 


Code
cat("Missing rows:", format(expected_rows - nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n")


Missing rows: 369,497 


Code
# Create complete panel
study_panel <- expand_grid(
  interval60 = unique(trips_panel$interval60),
  start_station = unique(trips_panel$start_station)
) %>%
  # Join trip counts
  left_join(trips_panel %>% 
              select(interval60, start_station, Trip_Count),
            by = c("interval60", "start_station")) %>%
  # Replace NA trip counts with 0
  mutate(Trip_Count = replace_na(Trip_Count, 0))

glimpse(study_panel)

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Why Temporal Lags?
If there were 15 bike trips from Station A at 8:00 AM, there will probably be ~15 trips at 9:00 AM. We can use this temporal persistence to improve predictions.
Note: We still can’t use spatial lags in prediction. Temporal lags are different because time moves in one direction - past predicts the future, but the future does not predict the past. Spatial lags are non-directional and create circular prediction logic.
Temporal lags: good Spatial lags: bad!


Code
# Sort by station and time
study_panel <- study_panel %>%
  arrange(start_station, interval60)

# Create lag variables WITHIN each station
study_panel <- study_panel %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  mutate(
    lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count, 1),
    lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 2),
    lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 3),
    lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 12),
    lag1day = lag(Trip_Count, 24)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station)
study_panel_complete <- study_panel %>%
  filter(!is.na(lag1day))

cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete), big.mark = ","), "\n")


Rows after removing NA lags: 542,556

# Plot actual vs lagged demand

  geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Why Temporal Validation Matters
In real operations, at 6:00 AM on March 15, we need to predict demand for March 15-31. We have data from Jan 1 - March 14, but NOT from March 15-31 (it hasn’t happened yet!).
Wrong approach: Train on weeks 10-13, test on weeks 1-9 (predicting past from future!)
Correct approach: Train on weeks 1-9, test on weeks 10-13 (predicting future from past)


Code
early_stations <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(date < as.Date("2025-06-01")) %>%
  filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>%
  distinct(start_station) %>%
  pull(start_station)

late_stations <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(date >= as.Date("2025-06-01")) %>%
  filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>%
  distinct(start_station) %>%
  pull(start_station)

common_stations <- intersect(early_stations, late_stations)

study_panel_complete <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(start_station %in% common_stations)

train <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(date < as.Date("2025-06-01"))

test <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(date >= as.Date("2025-06-01"))

cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(train), big.mark = ","), "\n")

cat("Testing observations:", format(nrow(test), big.mark = ","), "\n")


Testing observations: 179,280

The model uses Monday as the baseline. Each coefficient represents the difference in expected trips per station-hour compared to Monday - dow_simple2 = Tuesday..
Weekday Pattern (Tue-Fri):

Tuesday and Thursday have positive coefficients (+0.031 and +0.062), while Wednesday is almost the same as Monday (+0.002)
Friday is slightly below Monday (-0.016)
This suggests weekday ridership is fairly stable, but Thursday appears strongest and Friday is slightly weaker

Weekend Pattern (Sat-Sun):

Both weekend days have negative coefficients (-0.036 for Saturday and -0.087 for Sunday)
This means FEWER trips per station-hour than Monday

Hourly Interpretation
Overnight hours are the lowest, with hours 1–4 all below the midnight baseline. Ridership rises quickly after 6 AM, reaches a strong morning peak at 8 AM (+0.847), and stays elevated through midday. The strongest peak occurs at 5 PM (+1.188), showing an even stronger evening commute than morning commute.

Question: Did adding lags improve R²? Why or why not?
Yes, adding lag variables improved the model substantially. The R² increased from 0.112 in Model 1 to 0.309 in Model 2, which means the model explains much more variation in station-hour trips than before. This improvement makes sense because bike-share demand is strongly related to recent past demand. If a station was busy one hour ago, three hours ago, or at the same hour the previous day, it is more likely to be busy again now. These lag variables capture temporal persistence and repeated travel patterns, especially commuting behavior.

Station fixed effects capture persistent baseline differences across stations. In other words, some stations are simply busier than others because of their location and surrounding context, such as land use, transit connections, and neighborhood activity.

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Calculate Predictions and MAE


Code
# Get predictions on test set

# Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding
test <- test %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))

# Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables)
contrasts(test$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7)

test <- test %>%
  mutate(
    pred1 = predict(model1, newdata = test),
    pred2 = predict(model2, newdata = test),
    pred3 = predict(model3, newdata = test),
    pred4 = predict(model4, newdata = test),
    pred5 = predict(model5, newdata = test)
  )

# Calculate MAE for each model
mae_results <- data.frame(
  Model = c(
    "1. Time + Weather",
    "2. + Temporal Lags",
    "3. + Demographics",
    "4. + Station FE",
    "5. + Rush Hour Interaction"
  ),
  MAE = c(
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred1), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred2), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred3), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred4), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred5), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

kable(mae_results, 
      digits = 2,
      caption = "Mean Absolute Error by Model (Test Set)",
      col.names = c("Model", "MAE (trips)")) %>%

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Observed vs. Predicted
Let’s use our best model (Model 2) for error analysis.


Code
test <- test %>%
  mutate(
    error = Trip_Count - pred2,
    abs_error = abs(error),
    time_of_day = case_when(
      hour < 7 ~ "Overnight",
      hour >= 7 & hour < 10 ~ "AM Rush",
      hour >= 10 & hour < 15 ~ "Mid-Day",
      hour >= 15 & hour <= 18 ~ "PM Rush",
      hour > 18 ~ "Evening"
    )
  )

# Scatter plot by time and day type
ggplot(test, aes(x = Trip_Count, y = pred2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkgreen") +
  facet_grid(weekend ~ time_of_day) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs. Predicted Bike Trips",
    subtitle = "Model 2 performance by time period",
    x = "Observed Trips",
    y = "Predicted Trips",
    caption = "Red line = perfect predictions; Green line = actual model fit"
  ) +
  plotTheme









Question: Where is the model performing well? Where is it struggling?

The model performs best at low to moderate trip counts, where the points are more concentrated and the fitted green line follows the overall trend reasonably well



It seems to do relatively better overnight and during mid-day, when demand is lower and less variable



The model struggles more during PM Rush, where there are many high observed trip counts but the predicted values stay too low



This means the model tends to underpredict high-demand periods, especially during rush hours



In general, the farther the observed values move away from zero, the more the predictions flatten out below the red perfect-prediction line

Question: Do you see spatial clustering of errors? What neighborhoods have high errors?
Yes, the errors appear to be spatially clustered rather than randomly distributed. The highest prediction errors are concentrated in Center City and nearby parts of West Philadelphia. Outer stations appear to have both lower demand and lower error.

  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Median Income", x = "Median Income", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme

p2 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo, aes(x = Percent_Taking_Transit, y = MAE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Transit Usage", x = "% Taking Transit", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme

p3 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo, aes(x = Percent_White, y = MAE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Race", x = "% White", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme

(p1 | p2) / (p3 | plot_spacer())









Critical Question: Are prediction errors systematically higher in certain demographic groups? What are the equity implications?
The scatterplots suggest that prediction errors may be systematically related to neighborhood characteristics. MAE appears to increase with median income and with the percentage of White residents, while it decreases as transit usage increases. This means the model may be less accurate in some demographic contexts than others, rather than making equally sized errors everywhere. From an equity perspective, this matters because uneven model performance could lead to better service planning in some neighborhoods and worse planning in others.

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Compare results to Q1 2025:

Compared with the original Q1 template, Q2 appears to have higher overall ridership, which is consistent with warmer spring and early summer conditions. The same broad commute structure remains visible, with strong morning and evening peaks, but Q2 demand also appears more elevated across midday and afternoon periods. These seasonal differences may affect both model fit and prediction error, since warmer-weather travel can be more frequent and potentially more variable than winter ridership.

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Part 3: Feature Engineering & model improvement

Does this improve model fit?



Code
# 1. Add new features to the full panel
study_panel_fe <- study_panel_complete %>%
  arrange(start_station, interval60) %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  mutate(
    # Nice biking weather indicator
    nice_weather = ifelse(Temperature >= 60 & Temperature <= 75 & Precipitation == 0, 1, 0),
    
    # Weekend + nice weather interaction
    weekend_nice_weather = ifelse(weekend == 1 & nice_weather == 1, 1, 0),
    
    # Same station, same approximate hour one week earlier
    lag1week = lag(Trip_Count, 168)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# 2. Re-create train/test split for Q2
train_fe <- study_panel_fe %>%
  filter(date < as.Date("2025-06-01"))

test_fe <- study_panel_fe %>%
  filter(date >= as.Date("2025-06-01"))

# 3. Re-create day-of-week factor
train_fe <- train_fe %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))

test_fe <- test_fe %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))

# 4. Keep only rows with complete lag information
train_fe <- train_fe %>%
  drop_na(lag1Hour, lag3Hours, lag1day, lag1week)

test_fe <- test_fe %>%
  drop_na(lag1Hour, lag3Hours, lag1day, lag1week)

# 5. Fit improved linear model
model_improved <- lm(
  Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + lag1week +
    nice_weather + weekend_nice_weather +
    as.factor(start_station),
  data = train_fe
)


# 6. Predict on test set
test_fe <- test_fe %>%
  mutate(
    pred_improved = predict(model_improved, newdata = test_fe)
  )

# 7. Calculate MAE for improved model
mae_improved <- mean(abs(test_fe$Trip_Count - test_fe$pred_improved), na.rm = TRUE)

cat("Improved Model MAE:", round(mae_improved, 4), "\n")


Improved Model MAE: 0.675 


Code
# 8. Fit Poisson model for count data
poisson_model <- glm(
  Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + lag1week +
    nice_weather + weekend_nice_weather,
  family = poisson(link = "log"),
  data = train_fe
)


# 9. Predict from Poisson model
test_fe <- test_fe %>%
  mutate(
    pred_poisson = predict(poisson_model, newdata = test_fe, type = "response")
  )

# 10. Calculate MAE for Poisson model
mae_poisson <- mean(abs(test_fe$Trip_Count - test_fe$pred_poisson), na.rm = TRUE)

cat("Poisson Model MAE:", round(mae_poisson, 4), "\n")


Poisson Model MAE: 0.7229 


Code
best_old_mae <- 0.83

comparison_table <- tibble(
  Model = c("Best Previous Model", "Improved Linear Model", "Poisson Model"),
  MAE = c(best_old_mae, mae_improved, mae_poisson)
)

print(comparison_table)


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  Model                   MAE
  <chr>                 <dbl>
1 Best Previous Model   0.83 
2 Improved Linear Model 0.675
3 Poisson Model         0.723


Code
cat("\nRows in train_fe:", nrow(train_fe), "\n")



Rows in train_fe: 314985 


Code
cat("Rows in test_fe:", nrow(test_fe), "\n")


Rows in test_fe: 179280 


Code
cat("Train date range:", min(train_fe$date), "to", max(train_fe$date), "\n")


Train date range: 20187 to 20239 


Code
cat("Test date range:", min(test_fe$date), "to", max(test_fe$date), "\n")


Test date range: 20240 to 20269 


I added three new features based on the earlier error analysis:
nice_weather : an indicator for favorable biking weather, defined as temperatures between 60°F and 75°F with no precipitation
weekend_nice_weather: an interaction term to capture the idea that pleasant weather may increase leisure riding on weekends
lag1week: the trip count from the same station one week earlier, which helps capture repeated weekly demand patterns
These features were motivated by the patterns found in the earlier analysis. The temporal plots showed strong repeated demand cycles, which suggested that past demand from the same time last week could be useful. The Q2 context also made weather especially important, since spring and early summer biking is likely to be sensitive to pleasant conditions. In addition, weekend demand may be shaped by recreational travel, so a weekend-by-weather interaction was a reasonable extension.
The improved linear model reduced MAE from 0.830 in the previous best model to 0.675, which is a substantial improvement. This suggests that the new features captured important patterns that were missing from the earlier models.
I also estimated a Poisson model because trip counts are count data. The Poisson model achieved an MAE of 0.723, which was better than the previous best model but still worse than the improved linear model. This suggests that although the Poisson framework is conceptually appropriate for counts, the improved linear model performed better for prediction in this case.

title: Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction
text:
Critical Reflection
I think the final model is good enough to be useful for Indego, but not good enough to fully replace human decision-making. A final MAE of about 0.675 trips per station-hour is a clear improvement over the earlier models, which means the model is learning important demand patterns. Still, the model performs worse during PM rush and in the busiest stations, which is when prediction errors are most costly for rebalancing. If the model underpredicts demand at those times, stations may run out of bikes or docks when they are needed most. For that reason, I would recommend using this system as a support tool for operations staff, especially for identifying likely demand patterns, but not as a fully automatic deployment system.
I also think there are real equity concerns. The errors are not random: they are clustered in some parts of the city and appear to vary with demographic characteristics. That means some neighborhoods may receive less accurate demand forecasts than others, which could translate into less reliable bike access. If that happens, the system could reinforce existing inequalities instead of improving service fairly across the city. To prevent this, Indego should evaluate model performance by neighborhood, not only citywide, and should set safeguards such as regular fairness audits and manual review for high-error areas. The model is also limited because it misses factors like holidays, school schedules, special events, and real-time disruptions. With more time and better data, I would add those features and also incorporate more detailed information about station capacity and rebalancing operations.

section: Part 1: Healthcare Access for Vulnerable Populations
text:
Part 1: Healthcare Access for Vulnerable Populations

Research Question
Which Pennsylvania counties have the highest proportion of vulnerable populations (elderly + low-income) living far from hospitals?
Your analysis should identify counties that should be priorities for healthcare investment and policy intervention.
Your Task:
Questions to answer:

How many hospitals are in your dataset?
223
How many census tracts?
3445
What coordinate reference system is each dataset in?
pa_hospitals=WGS 84 ,“EPSG”,4326 pa_tracts=NAD83,“EPSG”,4269 pa_counties=WGS 84,“EPSG”,3857



Step 2: Get Demographic Data
Your Task:

# Get demographic data from ACS
library(tidycensus)
vars <- c(
  total_pop = "B01001_001",     
  med_income = "B19013_001",    
  m65_66 = "B01001_020", m67_69 = "B01001_021", m70_74 = "B01001_022", 
  m75_79 = "B01001_023", m80_84 = "B01001_024", m85_plus = "B01001_025",
  f65_66 = "B01001_044", f67_69 = "B01001_045", f70_74 = "B01001_046", 
  f75_79 = "B01001_047", f80_84 = "B01001_048", f85_plus = "B01001_049"
)
pa_census_data <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = vars,
  state = "PA",
  year = 2022, 
  output = "wide" 
)
pa_demographics <- pa_census_data %>%
  mutate(
    elderly_pop = rowSums(select(., m65_66E:f85_plusE)),
    elderly_pct = (elderly_pop / total_popE) * 100
  ) %>%
  rename(
    total_population = total_popE,
    median_income = med_incomeE
  ) %>%
  select(GEOID, NAME, total_population, median_income, elderly_pop, elderly_pct)

# Join to tract boundaries
pa_tracts_with_data <- pa_tracts %>%
  left_join(pa_demographics, by = "GEOID")

Questions to answer:

What year of ACS data are you using?
2022
How many tracts have missing income data?
62
What is the median income across all PA census tracts?
70188




Step 3: Define Vulnerable Populations
Your Task:

# Filter for vulnerable tracts based on your criteria
pa_valid_data <- pa_tracts_with_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(median_income)) %>%
  filter(total_population > 0)

income_limit <- 55924
elderly_limit<- 37.7


pa_vulnerable_populations <- pa_valid_data %>%
  mutate(
    is_vulnerable = ifelse(
      !is.na(median_income) & 
      median_income <= income_limit & 
      elderly_pct >= elderly_limit, 
      TRUE, 
      FALSE
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(is_vulnerable == TRUE)

Questions to answer:

What income threshold did you choose and why?
55924, I use the 75% quantile. quantile(pa_valid_data$elderly_pct,0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
What elderly population threshold did you choose and why?
37.7, I found that the 75th percentile is approximately 25%. This means this threshold helps me identify the top 25% of communities with the highest aging levels across the state. quantile(pa_valid_data$elderly_pct,0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
How many tracts meet your vulnerability criteria?
209
What percentage of PA census tracts are considered vulnerable by your definition?
209/3445=6.07%




Step 4: Calculate Distance to Hospitals
For each vulnerable tract, calculate the distance to the nearest hospital.
Your Task:

pa_vulnerable_projected <- st_transform(pa_vulnerable_populations, 2272)
pa_hospitals_projected <- st_transform(pa_hospitals, 2272)

vulnerable_centroids <- st_centroid(pa_vulnerable_projected)

distance<- st_distance(vulnerable_centroids,pa_hospitals_projected)

pa_vulnerable_projected$dist_to_hospital_miles <- as.numeric(apply(distance, 1, min)) / 5280

Questions to answer:

What is the average distance to the nearest hospital for vulnerable tracts?
3.538635
What is the maximum distance?
19.15822
How many vulnerable tracts are more than 15 miles from the nearest hospital?
7




Step 5: Identify Underserved Areas
Define “underserved” as vulnerable tracts that are more than 15 miles from the nearest hospital.
Your Task:

pa_vulnerable_projected<- pa_vulnerable_projected %>%
  mutate(is_underserved = dist_to_hospital_miles > 15)

Questions to answer:

How many tracts are underserved? 7
What percentage of vulnerable tracts are underserved? 7/209=3.34%
Does this surprise you? Why or why not? Yes, because there are almost 20,000 of population have to go to hospital that is more than 15 miles away. —



Step 6: Aggregate to County Level
Use spatial joins and aggregation to calculate county-level statistics about vulnerable populations and hospital access.
Your Task:

# Spatial join tracts to counties
pa_counties_projected <- st_transform(pa_counties, 2272)
vulnerable_with_county <- st_join(pa_vulnerable_projected, pa_counties_projected)

# Aggregate statistics by county
county_summary <- vulnerable_with_county %>%
  group_by(NAMELSADCO) %>%
  summarize(
    num_vulnerable_tracts=n(),
    num_underserved_tracts = sum(is_underserved),
    avg_distance=mean(dist_to_hospital_miles),
    total_vulnerable_population=sum(total_population,na.rm = TRUE))%>%
      mutate(
        pct_vul_tracts_underserved=((num_underserved_tracts / num_vulnerable_tracts)*100)
      )

Forest County, Juniata County, Monroe County, Sullivan County,Dauphin County

Forest County
Are there any patterns in where underserved counties are located?
Underserved counties are primarily concentrated in the rural, mountainous central and northern regions of PA, where low population density leads to sparse hospital coverage.

Using the skills from Week 3 (Data Visualization), create publication-quality maps and charts.

# Create county-level access map

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
The Policy Problem

Eviction filings are not evenly distributed across Philadelphia.
Limited rental assistance resources require a way to identify where preventive intervention may be most needed.

**This project asks whether eviction filing data can help identify high-risk census tracts before housing instability worsens.**

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
Research Question
Can recent eviction filing patterns help predict future eviction risk across Philadelphia census tracts?

We focus on three questions:

- Which tracts show persistent high filing activity?
- Does monthly or quarterly prediction work better?
- Can the model support rental assistance targeting?

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
Why Focus on March 2023–March 2026?
The model needs a stable policy environment.

Pandemic-era filing patterns were highly disrupted.
March 2023 onward shows more consistent monthly variation.
This period better reflects current eviction risk.
We use this period to train a more comparable forecasting model.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model

What This Distribution Means

Most tract-months have zero or very low filings.
A small number of observations have much higher filing counts.
This distribution supports using count-based models and high-risk classification.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
What the Spatial Pattern Shows

Eviction burden is not evenly distributed across Philadelphia.
Raw filing counts show where total eviction activity is highest.
Renter-adjusted rates show where eviction risk is most concentrated.
Tracts high on both measures are stronger candidates for rental assistance targeting.

Structural Housing Inequality

Black-majority tracts show higher average eviction filing levels.
Hispanic-majority and other-majority tracts also show higher filings than white-majority tracts.
These patterns suggest eviction risk reflects broader structural disparities in housing stability.
Model outputs should be interpreted with equity safeguards.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
Modeling Strategy
We test whether recent filing history can predict future tract-level eviction risk.

Monthly model: predicts next-month eviction filings.
Quarterly model: predicts next-quarter eviction filings.
Key predictors: lagged filings, rolling averages, historical baselines, and ACS housing variables.
Evaluation: prediction error, tract ranking, and high-risk classification.

We compare two forecasting scales because they serve different policy needs.

Shorter intervention window

Better for rapid outreach
More month-to-month noise


Quarterly

Smoother filing patterns
Better for broader planning
Less reactive to sudden spikes
Longer delay before intervention

Model Performance Summary

Recent filing history is highly predictive.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
section: Why Monthly Forecasting Is More Useful for Targeting
text:
Why Monthly Forecasting Is More Useful for Targeting

Rental assistance is often time-sensitive.
Monthly forecasts identify risk closer to when filings may occur.
Recent filing history and rolling averages capture short-term persistence.
This makes monthly prediction better suited for early outreach and prevention.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
section: The Model Captures Patterns but Misses Extremes
text:
The Model Captures Patterns but Misses Extremes

Predictions follow the general tract-level pattern.
The model compresses the upper tail of high-filing tracts.
Extreme filing spikes remain difficult to predict.
This supports using the model as a screening tool, not an automatic decision rule.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text: Quarterly Model Diagnostics

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
Quarterly Model Trade off
Quarterly aggregation smooths short-term noise, but it can hide sudden tract-level changes.

Quarterly predictions are less sensitive to monthly volatility.
This can help with general planning.
But the model struggles with extreme high-filing tracts.
For targeted rental assistance, this delay can be costly.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
Policy Application
The model is most useful as an early-warning and prioritization tool.

Identify tracts with elevated predicted filing risk.
Prioritize outreach before filings escalate.
Support rental assistance targeting at the neighborhood level.
Combine model output with local knowledge and equity review.

The model should support policy judgment, not replace it.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
Key Takeaways

Eviction filings in Philadelphia are spatially concentrated and structurally uneven.
Recent filing history is the strongest short-term predictor of future filings.
Forecasting models can help target rental assistance, but should be used with equity checks and policy judgment.

title: Philadelphia Eviction Forecasting Model
text:
Conclusion
Eviction risk is predictable enough to support early intervention, but not predictable enough to automate policy decisions.
A short-term forecasting model can help Philadelphia identify elevated-risk tracts and prioritize rental assistance outreach.
Its strongest use is not replacing human judgment, but making policy response faster, more targeted, and more transparent.

2.2 Data Quality Assessment
Your Task: Calculate margin of error percentages and create reliability categories.
Requirements: - Calculate MOE percentage: (margin of error / estimate) * 100 - Create reliability categories: - High Confidence: MOE < 5% - Moderate Confidence: MOE 5-10%
- Low Confidence: MOE > 10% - Create a flag for unreliable estimates (MOE > 10%)
Hint: Use mutate() with case_when() for the categories.

# Calculate MOE percentage and reliability categories using mutate()
income_analysis <- county_data_clean %>%
  mutate(
    moe_pct = (med_incomeM / med_incomeE) * 100,
    reliability = case_when(
      moe_pct < 5 ~ "High Confidence",
      moe_pct <= 10 ~ "Moderate Confidence",
      TRUE ~ "Low Confidence"
    ),
    unreliable_flag = if_else(moe_pct > 10, "Yes", "No")
  )

# Create a summary showing count of counties in each reliability category
reliability_summary <- income_analysis %>%
  count(reliability) %>%
  mutate(
    percentage = (n / sum(n)) * 100

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
5.1 Analysis Integration and Professional Summary
Your Task: Write an executive summary that integrates findings from all four analyses.
Executive Summary Requirements: 1. Overall Pattern Identification: What are the systematic patterns across all your analyses? 2. Equity Assessment: Which communities face the greatest risk of algorithmic bias based on your findings? 3. Root Cause Analysis: What underlying factors drive both data quality issues and bias risk? 4. Strategic Recommendations: What should the Department implement to address these systematic issues?
Executive Summary:
[Your integrated 4-paragraph summary here] 1. Across all four levels of analysis, a clear systematic pattern emerges: data reliability is inversely correlated with population density. At the county level, urban centers like Alameda exhibit high confidence with a margin of error (MOE) as low as 1.005%, whereas sparsely populated rural counties like Alpine suffer from low confidence with MOEs exceeding 17.2%. This trend persists at the tract level, where “High Uncertainty” areas have a significantly smaller average population (2,729) compared to “Reliable” tracts (4,435).

The communities facing the greatest risk of algorithmic bias are small, rural, and geographically isolated populations. Based on the pattern analysis, these “High Uncertainty” tracts often have a higher average percentage of White residents (48.83%) and significantly lower percentages of Black residents (0.83%) compared to urban centers. While urban minority communities benefit from the robust data of high-density areas, rural residents—regardless of race—are at risk of being “miscalculated” by algorithms that do not account for the high statistical noise inherent in small-sample data.
The primary driver of this bias risk is the inherent limitation of sampling in low-density areas. In a county like Alpine, which has a total population of only 1,515, the American Community Survey (ACS) sample size is so small that a few outlier responses can drastically skew the median income estimate. Algorithms treat these estimates as absolute facts, but in reality, they are “statistical flukes” caused by the geographic reality of rural life, creating a structural bias where data-rich urban areas receive more predictable and “fair” algorithmic treatment than data-sparse rural ones.
To mitigate these risks, the Department of Human Services should implement a “Reliability-Aware” Decision Framework. First, any community flagged with an MOE > 10% should be diverted from fully automated processing to a manual administrative review. Second, the Department should supplement Census data with local administrative records (such as state tax or utility data) for rural tracts to triangulate income estimates.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
Introduction
This analysis focuses on 311 service requests related to “Street Lights – All Out” in Chicago. These requests represent instances where entire street light fixtures are not functioning, which can affect visibility, public safety, and perceptions of neighborhood quality.
I selected this violation type because it reflects essential urban infrastructure and has clear spatial implications. Street lighting is closely tied to pedestrian safety, traffic conditions, and nighttime activity, making it an important service to analyze from a spatial perspective. Additionally, outages may cluster in specific areas due to aging infrastructure, maintenance patterns, or underlying neighborhood conditions.
By examining the spatial distribution and predictability of street light outages, this analysis aims to better understand how infrastructure-related issues are distributed across the city and whether spatial patterns can be used to improve prediction.


Part 1: Data Loading & Exploration

lights <- read_csv("C:/Users/yzhon/OneDrive - PennO365/Penn-Spring 2026/CPLN 592/cpln-5920-sp26-student-portfolio-template/Labs/Lab_4/311_Service_Requests_-_Street_Lights_-_All_Out_-_Historical_20260406.csv")

lights <- lights %>%
  mutate(`Creation Date` = mdy(`Creation Date`))

lights_2017 <- lights %>%
  filter(year(`Creation Date`) == 2017)

lights_2017_clean <- lights_2017 %>%
  filter(!is.na(Longitude), !is.na(Latitude))

lights_sf <- st_as_sf(
  lights_2017_clean,
  coords = c("Longitude", "Latitude"),
  crs = 4326
)

chicago <- st_read("https://data.cityofchicago.org/resource/igwz-8jzy.geojson",
                   quiet = TRUE)

chicago <- st_transform(chicago, st_crs(lights_sf))

plot(st_geometry(chicago), col = "grey90", border = "black")

plot(
  st_geometry(lights_sf),
  pch = 16,
  cex = 0.05,
  col = "#5B5F8D",
  add = TRUE
)








After filtering for 2017 and removing missing coordinates, the dataset contains approximately 14,587 valid observations. Only a small number of records were dropped due to missing spatial information, indicating that the dataset is largely complete.
The spatial distribution of street light outage requests in Chicago is clearly non-random and exhibits strong clustering patterns. High-density clusters are visible across multiple parts of the city, while other areas show relatively low levels of activity.
This uneven distribution suggests that street light outages are influenced by spatially varying factors such as infrastructure conditions, maintenance practices, or neighborhood characteristics. The presence of clustering implies spatial dependence, meaning that nearby locations tend to experience similar levels of service requests. This observation supports the use of spatial features and spatial modeling approaches in subsequent analysis.


Part 2: Fishnet Grid Creation

lights_proj <- st_transform(lights_sf, 26916)
chicago_proj <- st_transform(chicago, 26916)

grid <- st_make_grid(
  chicago_proj,
  cellsize = 500,
  square = TRUE
) %>%
  st_as_sf() %>%
  mutate(grid_id = row_number())

grid <- st_intersection(grid, chicago_proj)

joined <- st_join(grid, lights_proj, join = st_contains)

grid_counts <- joined %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(grid_id) %>%
  summarise(count = n()) %>%
  right_join(grid, by = "grid_id") %>%
  mutate(count = replace_na(count, 0)) %>%
  st_as_sf()

plot(
  grid_counts["count"],
  main = "Street Light Outage Counts per 500m Grid Cell"
)








To create a consistent unit of analysis, I generated a 500 meter by 500 meter fishnet grid covering Chicago and aggregated the 311 requests to each grid cell. This converts the original point-level data into count data at the grid level.
The resulting map shows that the distribution of street light outage requests is highly uneven. Most grid cells contain relatively few requests, while a smaller number of cells exhibit much higher counts. This indicates a right-skewed distribution typical of count data.
Additionally, high-count cells tend to cluster in specific areas rather than being randomly distributed. These clusters suggest the presence of spatial dependence and highlight areas where infrastructure issues may be more concentrated.


Part 3: Spatial Features

coords <- st_coordinates(st_centroid(grid_counts))

knn_5 <- get.knn(coords, k = 5)

grid_counts$knn_mean_count <- sapply(1:nrow(grid_counts), function(i) {
  mean(grid_counts$count[knn_5$nn.index[i, ]], na.rm = TRUE)
})

plot(
  grid_counts["knn_mean_count"],
  main = "Mean Count of 5 Nearest Neighbor Grid Cells"
)







knn_nb <- knearneigh(coords, k = 5)
nb <- knn2nb(knn_nb)
lw <- nb2listw(nb, style = "W")

local_moran <- localmoran(grid_counts$count, lw)

grid_counts$local_I <- local_moran[,1]
grid_counts$local_p <- local_moran[,5]

grid_counts$z_count <- scale(grid_counts$count)[,1]
grid_counts$lag_z <- lag.listw(lw, grid_counts$z_count)

grid_counts$cluster_type <- case_when(
  grid_counts$z_count > 0 & grid_counts$lag_z > 0 & grid_counts$local_p < 0.05 ~ "High-High",
  grid_counts$z_count < 0 & grid_counts$lag_z < 0 & grid_counts$local_p < 0.05 ~ "Low-Low",
  grid_counts$z_count > 0 & grid_counts$lag_z < 0 & grid_counts$local_p < 0.05 ~ "High-Low",
  grid_counts$z_count < 0 & grid_counts$lag_z > 0 & grid_counts$local_p < 0.05 ~ "Low-High",
  TRUE ~ "Not Significant"
)

grid_counts$cluster_type <- factor(
  grid_counts$cluster_type,
  levels = c("High-High", "High-Low", "Low-High", "Low-Low", "Not Significant")
)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = grid_counts, aes(fill = cluster_type), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Cluster Type",
    values = c(
      "High-High" = "#e41a1c",
      "High-Low" = "#f1a340",
      "Low-High" = "#92c5de",
      "Low-Low" = "#2166ac",
      "Not Significant" = "#e5e5e5"
    ),
    drop = FALSE
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Local Moran's I: Street Light Outage Clusters",
    subtitle = "High-High = Hot spots of street light outages"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.3),
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11)
  )







grid_counts$hotspot <- ifelse(grid_counts$cluster_type == "High-High", 1, 0)
grid_counts$coldspot <- ifelse(grid_counts$cluster_type == "Low-Low", 1, 0)

hotspots <- grid_counts %>% filter(hotspot == 1)

if (nrow(hotspots) > 0) {
  dist_mat <- st_distance(
    st_centroid(grid_counts),
    st_centroid(hotspots)
  )

  grid_counts$dist_to_hotspot <- apply(dist_mat, 1, min)
  grid_counts$dist_to_hotspot <- as.numeric(grid_counts$dist_to_hotspot)
} else {
  grid_counts$dist_to_hotspot <- NA
}

plot(
  grid_counts["dist_to_hotspot"],
  main = "Distance to Nearest Hotspot"
)








To capture spatial dependence, I constructed a k-nearest neighbor feature using the average count of the five nearest grid cells. This variable reflects the local intensity of nearby service requests.
I then computed Local Moran’s I using a spatial weights matrix based on the five nearest neighbors. The results identify statistically significant clusters of high and low values across the city.
The cluster classification shows that High-High areas (hotspots) are distributed across several parts of Chicago, indicating localized concentrations of street light outages. Low-Low areas (cold spots) are also present, suggesting regions with consistently low levels of reported outages.
Compared to a single dominant cluster, the hotspots appear relatively fragmented, indicating that outages are concentrated in multiple smaller areas rather than one continuous region. This suggests that infrastructure issues may vary at a fine spatial scale.
Finally, I calculated the distance from each grid cell to the nearest hotspot, providing a measure of proximity to areas with high outage intensity. This feature will be useful for predictive modeling in the next step.


Part 4: Count Regression Models

model_data <- grid_counts %>% st_drop_geometry()

model_data <- model_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(dist_to_hotspot))

pois_model <- glm(
  count ~ knn_mean_count + local_I + dist_to_hotspot,
  family = poisson(link = "log"),
  data = model_data
)

nb_model <- glm.nb(
  count ~ knn_mean_count + local_I + dist_to_hotspot,
  data = model_data
)

AIC(pois_model, nb_model) %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Model Comparison using AIC",
    digits = 2
  )


Model Comparison using AIC



df
AIC




pois_model
4
19262.19


nb_model
5
17850.46





I fitted both a Poisson regression model and a Negative Binomial model to predict the number of street light outage requests per grid cell. The independent variables include spatial features such as the mean count of neighboring cells, the Local Moran’s I statistic, and the distance to the nearest hotspot.
The results show that the Negative Binomial model provides a substantially better fit, with a much lower AIC value (17850.46) compared to the Poisson model (19262.19). This large difference indicates that the Poisson model is not appropriate for this dataset.
This outcome suggests that the count data exhibit significant overdispersion, where the variance exceeds the mean. The Negative Binomial model accounts for this property and therefore better captures the underlying distribution of street light outage requests.
Overall, the spatial features appear to be meaningful predictors of outage intensity. In particular, areas with higher neighboring counts and stronger spatial clustering tend to have higher numbers of requests, while distance to hotspots is negatively associated with request intensity.


Part 5: Spatial Cross-Validation (2017)

library(purrr)

coords <- st_coordinates(st_centroid(grid_counts))

set.seed(123)
grid_counts$group <- kmeans(coords, centers = 5, nstart = 20)$cluster

model_data <- grid_counts %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  filter(!is.na(dist_to_hotspot))

results <- map_dfr(sort(unique(model_data$group)), function(g) {
  
  train <- model_data %>% filter(group != g)
  test  <- model_data %>% filter(group == g)
  
  model <- glm.nb(
    count ~ knn_mean_count + local_I + dist_to_hotspot,
    data = train
  )
  
  pred <- predict(model, newdata = test, type = "response")
  
  tibble(
    group = g,
    mae = mean(abs(test$count - pred), na.rm = TRUE),
    rmse = sqrt(mean((test$count - pred)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
  )
})

results %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Spatial Cross-Validation Results by Group",
    digits = 2
  )

To evaluate predictive performance, I implemented a spatial cross-validation approach using Leave-One-Group-Out validation. The study area was divided into five spatial groups using k-means clustering based on grid centroids.
For each iteration, the Negative Binomial model was trained on four groups and tested on the remaining group. This approach ensures that training and testing data are spatially separated, providing a more realistic evaluation of model performance.
The results show an average MAE of 2.85 and an RMSE of 3.94. The RMSE is noticeably higher than the MAE, indicating the presence of some larger prediction errors in certain locations.
Additionally, model performance varies across spatial groups. For example, one group shows relatively low error (MAE ≈ 1.62), while another group has significantly higher error (MAE ≈ 3.76). This suggests that the model performs better in some areas than others.
Overall, the model demonstrates moderate predictive performance, but the variation in errors across space indicates that additional variables or spatial processes may be needed to improve prediction accuracy.


Part 6: Model Evaluation

baseline_mae <- mean(abs(model_data$count - model_data$knn_mean_count))
baseline_rmse <- sqrt(mean((model_data$count - model_data$knn_mean_count)^2))

baseline_mae

[1] 2.81013

baseline_rmse

[1] 3.985021

colMeans(results[, c("mae", "rmse")])

     mae     rmse 
2.847850 3.943987 


To evaluate the added value of the regression model, I compared its performance to a baseline based on spatial density, using the mean count of neighboring grid cells as a proxy for a KDE approach.
The baseline model achieves an MAE of 2.81 and an RMSE of 3.99, while the Negative Binomial model has an MAE of 2.85 and an RMSE of 3.94.
The results show that the baseline slightly outperforms the regression model in terms of MAE, indicating that simple spatial smoothing is already quite effective at capturing general patterns. However, the regression model achieves a lower RMSE, suggesting that it performs better in reducing larger prediction errors.
Overall, this comparison indicates that while spatial density alone provides strong predictive power, incorporating additional spatial features helps improve performance in more extreme or variable areas. This highlights the value of combining simple spatial structure with more complex modeling approaches.
